annotations.label: Compound propositions

anchor.snippet: Compound propositions are formed by connecting propositions by logical connectives

annotations.note: The reified `IProp` type (for the `itauto` tactic) builds compound formulas via its binary constructors, but it is intuitionistic meta-infrastructure, not a developed classical theory.

annotations.label: Propositional variables

anchor.snippet: in which propositions are represented by letters, which are called propositional variables

annotations.note: `IProp.var : Nat → IProp` reifies propositional atoms, but only as internal representation for the `itauto` decision procedure.

annotations.label: Classical truth-functional propositional logic

anchor.snippet: in which formulas are interpreted as having precisely one of two possible truth values

annotations.note: `Sat.Valuation` (`Nat → Prop`) gives a two-valued classical interpretation of atoms, but Mathlib has no named formalization of classical propositional logic as a system.

annotations.label: Propositional logic as a standard of logical consequence

anchor.snippet: is a specification of a standard of logical consequence

annotations.note: This is a high-level framing of what propositional logic is, with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Statement

anchor.snippet: which are defined as declarative sentences having truth value

annotations.note: The notion of a truth-bearing statement corresponds to Lean's core `Prop`, which is not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Examples of statements

anchor.snippet: Examples of statements might include

annotations.note: Illustrative natural-language examples with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Non-declarative sentences have no truth value

anchor.snippet: Such non-declarative sentences have no truth value

annotations.note: A linguistic distinction with no formal representation in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Compound sentences

anchor.snippet: Compound sentences are formed from simpler sentences and express relationships among the constituent sentences

annotations.note: `IProp`'s connective constructors form compound formulas from simpler ones, but only as intuitionistic tactic infrastructure.

annotations.label: Examples of compound sentences

anchor.snippet: Examples of such compound sentences might include

annotations.note: Natural-language examples with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Simple/atomic vs compound sentences

anchor.snippet: If sentences lack any logical connectives, they are called simple sentences

annotations.note: `IProp` distinguishes atoms (`.var`, `.true`, `.false`) from connective-built molecules, but only inside the `itauto` reification.

annotations.label: Sentential connectives

anchor.snippet: Sentential connectives are a broader category that includes logical connectives

annotations.note: This broader linguistic category is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Logical connective

anchor.snippet: is a kind of sentential connective with the characteristic feature that

annotations.note: Truth-functional connectives appear as the `and'`/`or`/`imp` constructors of `IProp` (and as Lean's `And`/`Or`/`→`), but there is no general definition of 'logical connective'.

annotations.label: Argument

anchor.snippet: An argument is defined as a pair of things

annotations.note: No Mathlib structure represents an argument as a (premise-set, conclusion) pair.

annotations.label: Example argument (modus ponens)

anchor.snippet: The following is an example of an argument within the scope of propositional logic

annotations.note: A specific illustrative argument, not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Modus ponens is classically valid

anchor.snippet: which is a classically valid form

annotations.note: Modus ponens is realized as the `app'` rule of the reified `Proof` type (and is just function application in Lean), but its validity is not proved as a semantic metatheorem.

annotations.label: Valid argument

anchor.snippet: An argument is valid if, and only if, it is necessary that

annotations.note: `Fmla.proof f c` (every valuation satisfying `f` satisfies `c`) is exactly semantic validity, but only for CNF formulas within the SAT-checking infrastructure.

annotations.label: Sound argument

anchor.snippet: An argument is sound if, and only if, it is valid and all its premises are true

annotations.note: The notion of a sound argument (valid plus true premises) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Symbolization of the example argument

anchor.snippet: would then be symbolized as follows

annotations.note: An illustrative symbolization with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Inference line (Gentzen notation)

anchor.snippet: The premises are shown above a line, called the inference line

annotations.note: Gentzen-style inference-line notation is not formalized as a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Syntactic consequence

anchor.snippet: The inference line represents syntactic consequence

annotations.note: The reified `Proof` type encodes derivability (syntactic consequence) for intuitionistic propositional logic, but not as a general relation with metatheorems.

annotations.label: Language of a propositional calculus

anchor.snippet: of a propositional calculus is defined in terms of

annotations.note: The `IProp` inductive type effectively defines a propositional language (atoms plus formation rules), though intuitionistic and for tactic use.

annotations.label: Well-formed formula

anchor.snippet: A well-formed formula is any atomic formula, or any formula that can be built up from atomic formulas

annotations.note: `IProp` is precisely the inductive type of well-formed propositional formulas, but it is intuitionistic reification used by `itauto`.

annotations.label: Recursive definition of a formula

anchor.snippet: a formula of propositional logic is defined recursively by these definitions

annotations.note: `IProp` is exactly such a recursive (inductive) definition of formulas, within the `itauto` tactic.

annotations.label: Definition 1: atoms are formulas

anchor.snippet: Atomic propositional variables are formulas

annotations.note: The `IProp.var` constructor realizes the base clause that atoms are formulas.

annotations.label: Definition 2: composition of formulas

anchor.snippet: is a sequence of m, possibly but not necessarily atomic

annotations.note: The `and'`/`or`/`imp` constructors realize the inductive clause that connectives applied to formulas yield formulas.

annotations.label: Definition 3: nothing else is a formula

anchor.snippet: Nothing else is a formula

annotations.note: The minimality ('no junk') of the inductive type `IProp` captures this closure clause automatically.

annotations.label: Examples of well-formed formulas

anchor.snippet: we have the following as examples of well-formed formulas

annotations.note: Illustrative example formulas, not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Principle of composition

anchor.snippet: is referred to by Colin Howson as the principle of composition

annotations.note: The inductive formation of `IProp` embodies the compositional principle, but the named principle itself is not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Molecules (molecular sentences)

anchor.snippet: Composite formulas (all formulas besides atoms) are called molecules

annotations.note: Composite (non-atomic) `IProp` values correspond to molecules, but there is no separate 'molecule' definition.

annotations.label: Closure Clause

anchor.snippet: It is sometimes called the Closure Clause

annotations.note: The closure clause is implicit in the inductive definition of `IProp`, not stated explicitly.

annotations.label: CF grammar in BNF

anchor.snippet: An alternative to the syntax definitions given above is to write a context-free (CF) grammar

annotations.note: The `IProp` inductive type is equivalent to such a context-free grammar, but no BNF grammar is written in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Propositional constants vs variables

anchor.snippet: Propositional constants represent some particular proposition

annotations.note: `IProp` distinguishes constants (`.true`, `.false`) from variables (`.var`), but the conceptual distinction is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Schemata (schematic letters)

anchor.snippet: Schemata, or schematic letters , however, range over all formulas

annotations.note: The notion of schematic metavariables ranging over formulas is not formalized.

annotations.label: Truth and falsity constants

anchor.snippet: some authors recognize only two

annotations.note: `IProp.true` and `IProp.false` are the two truth/falsity constants (mirroring Lean's `True`/`False`), within the reified syntax.

annotations.label: Two truth-values in classical logic

anchor.snippet: all propositions evaluate to exactly one of two truth-values

annotations.note: Classical bivalence is reflected in `Sat.Valuation` and in Lean's two-element `Bool`, but is not stated as a semantic principle.

annotations.label: Truth-value evaluation example

anchor.snippet: evaluates to False

annotations.note: An illustrative evaluation example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Bivalence, noncontradiction, excluded middle

anchor.snippet: are distinctive features of classical logic

annotations.note: Excluded middle (`Classical.em`) and noncontradiction are Lean-core theorems, not a Mathlib characterization of classical logic's distinctive features.

annotations.label: Interpretation (case)

anchor.snippet: is an assignment of semantic values to each formula

annotations.note: `Sat.Valuation` is an assignment of truth values to propositional variables (an interpretation), used in SAT certificate checking.

annotations.label: Boolean valuation

anchor.snippet: An interpretation that follows the rules of classical logic is sometimes called a Boolean valuation

annotations.note: `Sat.Valuation` together with `Valuation.satisfies` is exactly a Boolean valuation, but only within the tactic's CNF semantics.

annotations.label: Number of distinct interpretations

anchor.snippet: distinct propositional symbols there are

annotations.note: The count 2^n of interpretations is a cardinality fact (`card (Fin n → Bool)`) but is not stated about propositional interpretations.

annotations.label: Uncountably many interpretations

anchor.snippet: and therefore uncountably many distinct possible interpretations

annotations.note: Uncountability of `ℕ → Bool` follows from Cantor's theorem in Mathlib, but is not stated about propositional interpretations.

annotations.label: Counterexample and validity

anchor.snippet: where a counterexample is defined as a case

annotations.note: Validity as absence of a counterexample is exactly `Fmla.proof` (no satisfying valuation falsifies the conclusion), but only for CNF in the SAT infrastructure.

annotations.label: Truth-functionality of connectives

anchor.snippet: as the assumption of the truth-functionality of the connectives

annotations.note: `Valuation.satisfies` computes a clause's truth value compositionally from its literals, reflecting truth-functionality, but truth-functionality is not stated as a general principle.

annotations.label: Semantic definition via truth tables

anchor.snippet: the semantic definition of the connectives is usually represented as a truth table

annotations.note: Connective semantics are defined compositionally via `Valuation.satisfies`/`Valuation.neg` (and Lean's `Bool` operations), rather than via an explicit truth-table object.

annotations.label: Truth table of the five connectives

anchor.snippet: This table covers each of the main five logical connectives

annotations.note: The explicit five-connective truth table is an example display, not a Mathlib object (though `Bool` operations realize it).

annotations.label: Connectives defined via assignment expressions

anchor.snippet: the five connectives are defined as

annotations.note: `Valuation.satisfies`/`Valuation.neg` define connective semantics by recursive assignment expressions, but only for the CNF fragment.

annotations.label: Connectives definable in terms of others

anchor.snippet: Some of these connectives may be defined in terms of others

annotations.note: Mathlib proves specific interdefinability equivalences such as `imp_iff_not_or`, `not_and_or`, and `or_iff_not_imp_left`, which instantiate this general claim.

annotations.label: Truth-functionally complete systems

anchor.snippet: in the sense that all and only the classical propositional tautologies are theorems

annotations.note: Functional completeness / characterization of complete connective sets is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Only NOR and NAND are sole-sufficient

anchor.snippet: Besides NOR and NAND, no other connectives have this property

annotations.note: The result that only NOR and NAND are individually functionally complete is not formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalence/biconditional truth condition

anchor.snippet: are assigned the same semantic value under every interpretation

annotations.note: Lean's `Iff` is the biconditional and `iff_iff_implies_and_implies` characterizes it, but the 'same truth value under every interpretation' framing is not a separate decl.

annotations.label: Truth-in-a-case (model)

anchor.snippet: is called a model of

annotations.note: `Valuation.satisfies_fmla` expresses that a valuation is a model of a formula, but only for CNF formulas in the SAT infrastructure.

annotations.label: Falsity-in-a-case

anchor.snippet: This is the "truth of negation" definition of falsity-in-a-case

annotations.note: `Valuation.neg` defines a literal being falsified via the truth of its negation, matching this falsity-in-a-case notion at the literal level.

annotations.label: Semantic consequence

anchor.snippet: if there is no interpretation under which

annotations.note: `Fmla.proof f c` (every model of `f` is a model of `c`) is exactly semantic consequence, but restricted to CNF in the SAT infrastructure.

annotations.label: Valid formula (tautology)

anchor.snippet: if it is true under every interpretation

annotations.note: A tautology corresponds to a formula satisfied by every `Valuation`, but Mathlib has no general `Tautology`/valid-formula predicate.

annotations.label: Consistent sentence

anchor.snippet: is consistent if it is true under at least one interpretation

annotations.note: Satisfiability (truth under some valuation) is expressible via `∃ v, v.satisfies_fmla f`, but no named propositional satisfiability predicate exists (cf. first-order `IsSatisfiable`).

annotations.label: Complete case

anchor.snippet: is complete if, and only if, either

annotations.note: The notion of a complete case (deciding every formula) is not formalized for propositional logic.

annotations.label: Consistent case

anchor.snippet: is consistent if, and only if, there is no

annotations.note: The notion of a consistent case (no formula both true and false) is not formalized for propositional logic.

annotations.label: Classical logic semantic theorems

anchor.snippet: the following theorems apply

annotations.note: This unspecified bundle of semantic metatheorems about cases is not formalized.

annotations.label: Classification of proof systems

anchor.snippet: can be broadly classified into semantic proof systems and syntactic proof systems

annotations.note: A taxonomic distinction of proof systems with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Semantic proof systems

anchor.snippet: Semantic proof systems rely on the concept of semantic consequence

annotations.note: The general notion of a semantic proof system is not formalized.

annotations.label: Truth table (proof method)

anchor.snippet: A truth table is a semantic proof method used to determine the truth value

annotations.note: Although Lean's `decide` tactic performs truth-table-style evaluation, there is no Mathlib declaration formalizing truth tables as a proof method.

annotations.label: Semantic tableau

anchor.snippet: A semantic tableau is another semantic proof technique

annotations.note: Semantic tableaux are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Syntactic proof systems

anchor.snippet: focus on the formal manipulation of symbols according to specific rules

annotations.note: The general notion of a syntactic proof system is not formalized (the `itauto` `Proof` type is a specific instance, not this general definition).

annotations.label: Hilbert-style axiomatic system

anchor.snippet: is a set of axioms or assumptions from which other statements

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style axiomatic propositional system is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Natural deduction

anchor.snippet: Natural deduction is a syntactic method of proof that emphasizes the derivation of conclusions

annotations.note: The reified `Proof` type uses natural-deduction-style introduction/elimination rules (`intro`, `andIntro`, `orElim'`, ...), but it is intuitionistic tactic infrastructure, not a defined ND system.

annotations.label: Sequent calculus

anchor.snippet: The sequent calculus is a formal system that represents logical deductions

annotations.note: Sequent calculus is not formalized as a system in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proving validity via truth table

anchor.snippet: it is possible to prove a formula's validity by using a truth table

annotations.note: The truth-table method for proving validity is realized in practice by `decide`, but not formalized as a metatheoretic claim.

annotations.label: Validity iff all lines true

anchor.snippet: If, and only if, all the lines of a truth table come out true, the formula is semantically valid

annotations.note: The equivalence of validity with all truth-table rows being true is not stated as a Mathlib theorem.

annotations.label: Truth table showing non-validity

anchor.snippet: For instance, this table shows that

annotations.note: An illustrative truth table, not formalized.

annotations.label: Semantic consequence via truth table

anchor.snippet: we can use a truth table to prove that a formula is a semantic consequence

annotations.note: Using truth tables to establish semantic consequence is not formalized as a method/theorem.

annotations.label: Analytic tableaux

anchor.snippet: Analytic tableaux are a more efficient, but nevertheless mechanical

annotations.note: Analytic tableaux are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Signed formula

anchor.snippet: where a signed formula is an expression

annotations.note: Signed formulas (tableau method) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Closing of branches and trees

anchor.snippet: the branch is said to close

annotations.note: The closing of tableau branches/trees is not formalized.

annotations.label: Closed tree proves self-contradiction

anchor.snippet: a closed tree is a proof that the original formula

annotations.note: The tableau correctness claim for self-contradiction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tableau proves tautology

anchor.snippet: a tableau can also prove that a logical formula is tautologous

annotations.note: The tableau method for proving tautologies is not formalized.

annotations.label: Closed tableau proves argument validity

anchor.snippet: A closed tree will be proof that the argument was valid

annotations.note: Tableau soundness for argument validity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Classically valid argument forms

anchor.section: List of classically valid argument forms
anchor.snippet: the following classical argument forms are semantically valid

annotations.note: Individual forms correspond to Lean lemmas (e.g. modus tollens `mt`, `Or.resolve_left`) and are all provable by `itauto`, but no Mathlib theorem enumerates them as valid argument forms.

annotations.label: Natural deduction specified by inference rules

anchor.snippet: is specified by providing inference rules

annotations.note: The `Proof` inductive type specifies a natural-deduction calculus via its rule-constructors, but intuitionistically and for tactic use.

annotations.label: Proof (Suppes–Lemmon notation)

anchor.snippet: is a sequence of lines containing sentences

annotations.note: Line-based Suppes–Lemmon proofs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ten primitive rules of proof

anchor.snippet: There are ten primitive rules of proof

annotations.note: This specific set of ten primitive natural-deduction rules is not formalized.

annotations.label: Natural deduction proof example (MTT derivable)

anchor.snippet: which shows that MTT is not a primitive rule

annotations.note: A worked derivation example, not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Axiomatic proof

anchor.snippet: It is possible to perform proofs axiomatically

annotations.note: Axiomatic (Hilbert-style) proof for propositional logic is not formalized.

annotations.label: Frege's six axioms

anchor.snippet: It had six axioms

annotations.note: Frege's specific six axioms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Frege's system is complete and consistent

anchor.snippet: to yield a complete and consistent axiomatization of classical truth-functional propositional logic

annotations.note: Completeness/consistency of Frege's axiomatization is not formalized.

annotations.label: Third axiom is superfluous (Łukasiewicz)

anchor.snippet: the third axiom is superfluous since it can be derived from the preceding two axioms

annotations.note: The derivability of Frege's third axiom from the others is not formalized.

annotations.label: Łukasiewicz's three axioms (P2)

anchor.snippet: with this system of three axioms

annotations.note: The Łukasiewicz P2 axiom system is not formalized.

annotations.label: Schematic form of P2

anchor.snippet: the axioms are given as

annotations.note: The schematic axiom forms of P2 are not formalized.

annotations.label: Proof example in P2

anchor.snippet: As an example, a proof of

annotations.note: A worked axiomatic proof example, not formalized.

annotations.label: Soundness

anchor.snippet: Relative to any of the standard types of proof systems mentioned above, it is sound

annotations.note: Soundness of a propositional proof system relative to classical semantics is not formalized as a metatheorem.

annotations.label: Strong completeness

anchor.snippet: It is also complete , indeed strongly complete for arbitrary sets of premises

annotations.note: Strong completeness of classical propositional logic is not formalized (Mathlib lacks even a first-order completeness theorem).

annotations.label: Theorem iff logically valid

anchor.snippet: a formula is a theorem if and only if it is logically valid

annotations.note: The completeness equivalence (theorem iff valid) is not formalized for propositional logic.

annotations.label: Compactness

anchor.snippet: A further central result is compactness

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the compactness theorem for first-order logic, which generalizes propositional compactness, but the propositional case is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Consistency coincides with satisfiability

anchor.snippet: syntactic consistency coincides with satisfiability

annotations.note: The equivalence of syntactic consistency and satisfiability requires a completeness theorem, which Mathlib does not have for propositional (or first-order) logic.

annotations.label: Decidability

anchor.snippet: Classical propositional logic is also decidable

annotations.note: Decision procedures exist (`itauto`, plus Lean's `decide` on `Decidable` connectives), but decidability is not stated as a metatheorem.

annotations.label: Satisfiability is decidable

anchor.snippet: satisfiability of a propositional formula is decidable

annotations.note: Mathlib's `FromLRAT` checks SAT certificates but does not prove that satisfiability is decidable.

annotations.label: SAT is NP-complete

anchor.snippet: Deciding satisfiability of propositional logic formulas is an NP-complete problem

annotations.note: NP-completeness of SAT (Cook–Levin) is not formalized in Mathlib.
